Large parts of this manual originate from Travis E. Oliphant’s book Guide to NumPy(https://archive.org/details/NumPyBook) (which generously entered Public Domain in August 2008). The reference documentation for many of the functions are written by numerous contributors and developers of NumPy.

sin(#page=1394)  (x, /[, out, where, casting, order, ...])             Trigonometric sine, element-wise.

arcsinh(#page=1420) is a multivalued function: for each x there are infinitely many numbers z such that sinh(z) = x. The        convention is to return the z whose imaginary part lies in [-pi/2, pi/2].
sinh(#page=1417)  (x, /[, out, where, casting, order, ...])            Hyperbolic sine, element-wise.
tanh(#page=1419)  (x, /[, out, where, casting, order, ...])            Compute hyperbolic tangent element-wise.
d2 = abs(a - mean)**2  # abs is for complex `a`
fabs(#page=1520)  (x, /[, out, where, casting, order, ...])            Compute the absolute values element-wise.
negative(#page=1479)    (x, /[, out, where, casting, order, ...])      Numerical negative, element-wise.
around(#page=1428) is an alias of round(#page=1426).
logical_not(#page=1381)      (x, /[, out, where, casting, ...])        Compute the truth value of NOT x element-wise.
log2(#page=1462)  (x, /[, out, where, casting, order, ...])            Base-2 logarithm of  x.
log10(len(test_elements))  >  (log10(max(test_elements)-min(test_elements))      - 2.27) / 0.927, but may use greater memory. The default value for kind will be automatically selected

arcsin(#page=1398) is a multivalued function: for each x there are infinitely many numbers z such that sin(z) = x. The        convention is to return the angle z whose real part lies in [-pi/2, pi/2].
arccos(#page=1400) is a multivalued function: for each x there are infinitely many numbers z such that cos(z) = x. The        convention is to return the angle z whose real part lies in [0, pi].
arctanh(#page=1424) is a multivalued function: for each x there are infinitely many numbers z such that tanh(z) = x.
subtract(#page=1485)    (x1, x2, /[, out, where, casting, ...])        Subtract arguments, element-wise.
multiply(#page=1480)     (x1, x2, /[, out, where, casting, ...])       Multiply arguments element-wise.
less_equal(#page=1391)     (x1, x2, /[, out, where, casting, ...])     Return the truth value of (x1 <= x2) element-wise.
less(#page=1390)  (x1, x2, /[, out, where, casting, ...])              Return the truth value of (x1 < x2) element-wise.
logical_xor(#page=1382)      (x1, x2, /[, out, where, ...])            Compute the truth value of x1 XOR x2, element-wise.
hypot(#page=1407)   (x1, x2, /[, out, where, casting, ...])            Given the "legs" of a right triangle, return its hypotenuse.
true_divide(#page=1486)      (x1, x2, /[, out, where, ...])            Divide arguments element-wise.
true_divide(#page=1486)      (x1, x2, /[, out, where, ...])            Divide arguments element-wise.
mod(#page=1491)  (x1, x2, /[, out, where, casting, order, ...])        Returns the element-wise remainder of division.

NumPy uses the IEEE Standard for Binary Floating-Point for Arithmetic (IEEE 754). This means that Not a        Number is not equivalent to infinity.

where seq would be a NumPy array of double values, and its length n would be extracted from seq internally before being passed to the C routine. Even better, since NumPy supports construction of arrays from arbitrary Python sequences, seq itself could be a nearly arbitrary sequence (so long as each element can be converted to a double) and the wrapper code would internally convert it to a NumPy array before extracting its data and length.

A basic format in this context is an optional shape specifier followed by an array-protocol type string. Parenthesis       are required on the shape if it has more than one dimension. NumPy allows a modification on the format in that any       string that can uniquely identify the type can be used to specify the data-type in a field. The generated data-type      fields are named 'f0', 'f1', …, 'f<N-1>' where N (>1) is the number of comma-separated basic formats        in the string. If the optional shape specifier is provided, then the data-type for the corresponding field describes a       sub-array.

A field is a view of the array data with a given data-type. The values in the view are determined by the given             type and the offset into the current array in bytes. The offset needs to be such that the view dtype fits in the             array dtype; for example an array of dtype complex128 has 16-byte elements. If taking a view with a 32-bit             integer (4 bytes), the offset needs to be between 0 and 12 bytes.
